desc: The classic 'roly-poly.' Introduced. Rolls into a perfect ball when disturbed. The most familiar terrestrial crustacean.

fm.Defense: Rolls into tight, seamless ball (conglobation) — diagnostic
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; detritivore; under pots, rocks, mulch; gregarious
fm.Habitat: Gardens, anywhere with moisture and organic debris

hp: Most familiar terrestrial isopod. Detritivore — consumes leaf litter, dead wood. Rolls into ball (conglobation).

desc: Introduced. Rough-textured, cannot roll into ball (unlike pillbug). Runs when disturbed. The most common 'sowbug.'

fm.Color: Slate gray; rough tuberculate surface; cannot roll into ball
fm.Defense: Runs (cannot conglobate); tucks legs under body
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal detritivore; under boards, pots, bark; gregarious
fm.Habitat: Gardens, basements, wood piles — any moist shelter

hp: Most common woodlouse worldwide. Detritivore under debris. Cannot roll up — runs when disturbed.

desc: Dalmatian pillbug — strikingly patterned black-and-white/yellow spotted pillbug. Extremely popular in the isopod-keeping hobby. Rolls into tight ball when disturbed (conglobation). European introduction. Under rocks and logs in relatively dry microhabitats. Slow-growing.

fm.Color: Dark with bold white/cream spots or stripes — 'zebra' pattern
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; under bark, rocks; detritivore; popular hobby species

hp: Detritivore in leaf litter. Spotted pattern. Popular in isopod-keeping hobby. Rolls into ball.

desc: Dilated woodlouse — one of the largest terrestrial isopods in LA County (18-20mm). Broad, flat body. Under large rocks in foothill habitats. Distinguished from P. scaber by much larger size and broader body. European introduction. Detritivore in leaf litter.

fm.Size: ½ in–¾ in (large for a woodlouse)
fm.Behavior: Under large rocks and logs; foothill canyons

hp: Detritivore in leaf litter — nutrient cycling. Porcellionidae.

desc: Native amphipod (not true isopod) of wrack lines on LA beaches. Jumps vigorously when disturbed. Essential decomposer of beach kelp.

fm.Defense: Explosive jumping — can leap 20+ body lengths
fm.Behavior: Burrows in sand under wrack line; nocturnal; jumps when disturbed

hp: Beach wrack decomposer — breaks down stranded kelp and seaweed. Coastal nutrient cycling. Beach and dune habitat.

desc: Powder blue isopod — fast-running woodlouse with distinctive pale powder-blue/gray pruinose coating. Very common in compost, garden debris, and greenhouses. Introduced. Smaller and much faster than Armadillidium — runs rapidly rather than rolling up. Popular in terrarium hobby.

fm.Color: Blue-gray with pale powdery coating; smooth
fm.Behavior: Faster than other woodlice; attracted to warm compost; gregarious

hp: Found in and around buildings and structures. Garden associate.

desc: Nosy pillbug — distinguished from Common Pillbug by pointed triangular frontal plate ('nose'). Often darker than A. vulgare. Rolls into ball. European introduction, less common than A. vulgare. Under rocks and logs in gardens. The pointed rostrum is diagnostic.

fm.Behavior: Like A. vulgare; distinguished by head shape

hp: Terrestrial isopod — detritivore in leaf litter. Calcium-dependent. Nutrient cycling. In gardens and urban areas.

desc: Federally Endangered. Breeds in ephemeral vernal pools. Eggs survive years in dry soil. LA County populations at extreme northwestern range edge.

fm.Color: Translucent; orange to reddish; swims upside-down
fm.Defense: Eggs (cysts) survive years of drought in dry soil
fm.Behavior: Swims upside-down in temporary rain pools; filter feeder
fm.Habitat: Vernal pools — ephemeral rain pools on hardpan clay
fm.Season: Appears after winter rain fills pools; Jan–Apr

hp: FEDERALLY ENDANGERED (63 FR 52740, 1998). San Diego fairy shrimp. Vernal pool obligate. LA County at range edge.

desc: Rock louse — large (25mm), fast marine isopod of rocky shorelines. Sprints across intertidal rocks with remarkable speed and agility — one of the fastest isopods. Large compound eyes. Feeds on algae and detritus. Cannot roll up. Native. Nocturnal, hiding in crevices by day.

fm.Color: Gray-brown; very fast; large dark eyes
fm.Defense: Extreme speed; darts into rock crevices
fm.Behavior: Fastest isopod; sprints across intertidal rocks; nocturnal; gregarious
fm.Habitat: Rocky intertidal — jetties, sea walls, rocky shores

hp: Rocky intertidal isopod — sprints across wave-washed rocks. Feeds on stranded algae, diatoms, decomposing organic matter. Important shorebird prey.

desc: Federally Threatened. Small translucent crustacean of vernal pools. Indicator of this critically endangered habitat.

fm.Behavior: Filter feeds in temporary pools; swims upside-down

hp: FEDERALLY THREATENED (59 FR 48136, 1994). Vernal pool fairy shrimp. Ephemeral rain pool specialist. Critical habitat designated.

desc: Common shiny woodlouse — medium gray oval isopod with glossy smooth body. Cannot roll into ball (unlike Armadillidium). European introduction. Under logs, stones, and in leaf litter. Requires moist habitat. Distinguished by smooth shiny texture and inability to conglobate.
hp: Terrestrial isopod — detritivore in leaf litter. Calcium-dependent. Nutrient cycling. Oniscidae.

desc: Fast-running oniscid isopod with distinctive dark dorsal stripe on shiny brown body. Introduced from Europe. In leaf litter, compost, and moist garden debris. Much faster than Armadillidium or Porcellio — runs rapidly when disturbed rather than rolling up. Hygrophilic — needs consistently moist microhabitat.
hp: Common striped woodlouse — fast-running isopod in garden leaf litter. Detritivore shredding fallen leaves. European introduction.

desc: Pink woodlouse — tiny (6mm) translucent pink isopod. European introduction originally from caves — retains cave-adapted pale coloring. In damp leaf litter, cellars, and under deeply buried stones. The distinctive pink coloring makes identification immediate.

fm.Habitat: Leaf litter, under stones — European introduction

hp: Cave/crevice specialist — adapted to dark, stable microhabitats.

desc: Pill woodlouse — large pillbug (up to 18mm), bigger than the common Armadillidium vulgare. Mediterranean introduction. Can roll into a very tight ball. Sandy, well-drained soils — less moisture-dependent than many isopods. Historically used in folk medicine (hence 'officinalis').
Tiny pink-purple woodlouse in damp leaf litter. European. Often overlooked due to small size. Parthenogenetic populations.
hp: Terrestrial isopod — detritivore in leaf litter. Calcium-dependent. Nutrient cycling. Trichoniscidae.

desc: Large marine isopod on exposed rocky coast. Fast-moving. Important intertidal decomposer and prey for shorebirds.

Smooth woodlouse — medium-large, dark shiny isopod with characteristically smooth/polished dorsal surface and pale lateral margins. Very common in gardens, under pots, around buildings. European introduction. Cannot roll up. Fast runner. Popular feeder isopod for reptile keepers.

fm.Color: Smooth dark with pale lateral margins

hp: Found in and around buildings and structures. Garden associate.

desc: Fast-moving woodlouse with dark median stripe. Runs quickly when uncovered — much faster than pill bugs. Leaf litter specialist.

fm.Color: Brown with dark median stripe; fast runner

hp: Terrestrial isopod — detritivore in leaf litter. Calcium-dependent. Nutrient cycling. Philosciidae.
